title: Die Geschichte vom bösen Friederich
source: Heinrich Hoffmann, Der Struwwelpeter, Rütten & Loening, Frankfurt am Main, 564. Auflage — the canonical later text in modernized spelling (via Project Gutenberg #24571; the ebook’s transcription slip »wir bös er war« corrected to the universal printed reading »wie bös er war«). Fresh Lectorium translation from the German.
about: The book’s first full story: cruel Friederich, who torments animals and even whips his Gretchen — until the big dog he attacks bites back. Justice in Struwwelpeter is exact and delivered by the victim: while Friederich lies in bed taking bitter medicine, the dog sits at his little table and eats his dinner, the confiscated whip kept carefully at hand.

sentences.t:
Der Friederich, der Friederich
das war ein arger Wüterich!
sentences.l:
The Friederich, the Friederich,
that was a wicked rage-fiend!
sentences.i:
Friederich, Friederich —
what a vicious brute he was!
sentences.n: The article before the name (»der Friederich«, like »der Konrad«), doubled into a chant. »der Wüterich« = ‘a raging brute, a violent tyrant,’ built on »Wut« (rage) with the old person-making suffix -rich — which happens to rhyme with the name itself, and the poem milks it. »arg« = ‘bad, wicked’ (old-fashioned).

sentences.t:
Er fing die Fliegen in dem Haus
und riß ihnen die Flügel aus.
sentences.l:
He caught the flies in the house
and tore to-them the wings out.
sentences.i:
He caught the flies about the house
and tore their wings off.
sentences.n: »ausreißen« is separable: »riß … aus« = ‘tore out.’ And the dative where English uses a possessive: »riß ihnen die Flügel aus« — ‘tore the wings out on them’ = ‘tore their wings out’ (the same pattern as Grimm’s »kämm uns die Haare«). »riß« is the pre-reform spelling of »riss«.

sentences.t:
Er schlug die Stühl und Vögel tot,
die Katzen litten große Not.
sentences.l:
He struck the chairs and birds dead,
the cats suffered great distress.
sentences.i:
He beat the chairs and birds to death;
the cats went through torments.
sentences.n: »totschlagen« is separable: »schlug … tot« = ‘struck dead.’ Killing birds — and, absurdly, chairs — in a single breath is the poem’s dark comedy. »Stühl« = »Stühle«, clipped for the meter (like »Scher« and »Stub« in the Daumenlutscher). »Not« = ‘distress, misery.’

sentences.t:
Und höre nur, wie bös er war:
Er peitschte seine Gretchen gar!
sentences.l:
And hear only, how bad he was:
He whipped his Gretchen even!
sentences.i:
And just listen how wicked he was:
he even whipped his Gretchen!
sentences.n: »nur« after an imperative = ‘just (listen)’ — the sibling of »einmal« in »sieh einmal«. »gar« at the very end = ‘even, no less’: the climax marker. »Gretchen« (little Grete) is the girl who looks after him in the illustrations — whipping *her* tops even the animal cruelty. Textual note: the Gutenberg ebook misprints »wir bös«; every printed edition reads »wie bös«, restored here.

sentences.t:
Am Brunnen stand ein großer Hund,
trank Wasser dort mit seinem Mund.
sentences.l:
At-the well stood a big dog,
drank water there with his mouth.
sentences.i:
By the well there stood a big dog,
drinking water there with his mouth.
sentences.n: »der Brunnen« — the well or fountain. The second line simply drops the subject (»trank …« = ‘[and] drank …’) — verse can chain verbs onto one subject without ‘and.’ Drinking ‘with his mouth’ is the poem’s deadpan: what else would he drink with? The innocence of the scene sets up the ambush.

sentences.t:
Da mit der Peitsch herzu sich schlich
der bitterböse Friederich;
sentences.l:
There with the whip up-to himself crept
the bitter-wicked Friederich;
sentences.i:
Then up crept, whip in hand,
the bitterly wicked Friederich;
sentences.n: »herzu« = ‘up to the spot, hither’ — an old-flavored directional adverb. »sich schleichen« = ‘to creep, sneak.’ And German saves the subject for last: the whole clause unwinds before »der bitterböse Friederich« finally slinks into it. »bitterböse« = wicked through and through; »Peitsch« = »Peitsche«, clipped.

sentences.t:
und schlug den Hund, der heulte sehr,
und trat und schlug ihn immer mehr.
sentences.l:
and struck the dog, who howled sorely,
and kicked and struck him always more.
sentences.i:
and he whipped the dog, who howled,
and kicked and beat him more and more.
sentences.n: »sehr« in its old sense again — ‘sorely’ (exactly as in »da schreit der Konrad sehr«). »der heulte …« — »der« as a demonstrative pronoun picking up the dog: ‘who howled.’ »immer mehr« = ‘more and more.’

sentences.t:
Da biß der Hund ihn in das Bein,
recht tief bis in das Blut hinein.
sentences.l:
Then bit the dog him in the leg,
right deep till into the blood in.
sentences.i:
Then the dog bit him in the leg,
deep, right down to the blood.
sentences.n: German bites you ‘in the leg’ with the definite article (»in das Bein«) — no possessive, as always with body parts. »bis in … hinein« = ‘right into’: the »hinein« doubles the »in« for direction. »biß« is the pre-reform spelling of »biss«.

sentences.t:
Der bitterböse Friederich,
der schrie und weinte bitterlich.
sentences.l:
The bitter-wicked Friederich,
he screamed and wept bitterly.
sentences.i:
And the bitterly wicked Friederich —
he screamed and wept bitterly.
sentences.n: The fronted name plus the resumptive »der« (‘Friederich — *he* screamed…’) is spoken-German emphasis. And hear the echo: the »bitterböse« boy now weeps »bitterlich« — the poem turns his own adjective against him.

sentences.t:
Jedoch nach Hause lief der Hund
und trug die Peitsche in dem Mund.
sentences.l:
However to home ran the dog
and carried the whip in the mouth.
sentences.i:
But the dog ran off home,
carrying the whip in his mouth.
sentences.n: »jedoch« = ‘however’ — mock-formal for a dog’s exit. »nach Hause« with the dative -e, where the Daumenlutscher had clipped »nach Haus« — both were current; meter decides. The dog confiscating the whip is the story’s hinge.

sentences.t:
Ins Bett muß Friedrich nun hinein,
litt vielen Schmerz an seinem Bein;
sentences.l:
Into the bed must Friedrich now in,
suffered much pain on his leg;
sentences.i:
Now Friedrich has to go to bed,
and suffered great pain in his leg;
sentences.n: »Ins Bett muß Friedrich nun hinein« — the motion verb is simply left out after »muß« (‘must [go] in’), just as after »wollen« in Grimm; the »hinein« carries the direction alone. This edition prints »Friedrich« (without the middle -e-) in these last stanzas — same boy, stricter meter. Note the tense-jump: present »muß«, then past »litt« — verse licence, the scene vivid and then narrated.

sentences.t:
und der Herr Doktor sitzt dabei
und gibt ihm bittre Arzenei.
sentences.l:
and the Mister Doctor sits thereby
and gives him bitter physic.
sentences.i:
and the doctor sits at his bedside
and gives him bitter medicine.
sentences.n: »der Herr Doktor« — title with article and honorific, the period’s respectful style. »die Arzenei« is the archaic, meter-stretched form of »Arznei« (‘medicine, physic’); »bittre« = »bittere«. The bitter medicine answers the bitter tears of the stanza before.

sentences.t:
Der Hund an Friedrichs Tischchen saß,
wo er den großen Kuchen aß;
sentences.l:
The dog at Friedrich’s little-table sat,
where he the big cake ate;
sentences.i:
The dog sat at Friedrich’s little table,
and there he ate the big cake;
sentences.n: »das Tischchen« — the -chen diminutive (as in Grimm’s »Töpfchen«): the sick child’s own little table. Verb-final »saß« and »aß« in their clauses — and the revenge scene begins: the victim takes the patient’s place.

sentences.t:
aß auch die gute Leberwurst
und trank den Wein für seinen Durst.
sentences.l:
ate also the good liver-sausage
and drank the wine for his thirst.
sentences.i:
he ate the good liverwurst too,
and drank the wine to quench his thirst.
sentences.n: »die Leberwurst« — liver sausage, the classic sickroom treat (meant for Friedrich). »für seinen Durst« = ‘for his thirst,’ i.e. to quench it. The subject is dropped again — the verbs simply keep chaining onto the dog.

sentences.t:
Die Peitsche hat er mitgebracht
und nimmt sie sorglich sehr in acht.
sentences.l:
The whip has he along-brought
and takes it, carefully, much in care.
sentences.i:
He has brought the whip along with him
and keeps it very carefully safe.
sentences.n: »mitbringen« separable — »hat … mitgebracht« = ‘has brought along,’ as in Grimm. »etwas in acht nehmen« = ‘to mind, look after something’ (printed lowercase »in acht« in this edition); »sorglich« is the old form of »sorgfältig«, ‘carefully.’ The dog keeps the whip — in case. No adult ever appears to punish Friederich; the poem doesn’t need one.
